coredata.dc:title: Effect of nanoparticle size on the mechanical properties of polymer nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hao
Zhu, Hanqi
Xu, Chunning
Li, Yunpeng
Liu, Qiang
Wang, Shaojuan
Yan, Shouke
coredata.dc:description: The mechanical properties of a series of polymer nanocomposites (PNCs) of polyvinylacetate (PVAc) and carbon dots (CDs) with average diameters of 2.5 and 8.5 nm (denoted as CD-2.5 and CD-8.5, respectively) or carbon black (N330) were investigated. The results show that when the nanoparticle size larger than the tube diameter of PVAc, e.g. N330 and CD-8.5, the complex viscosity, reptation relaxation time, and elongation at break of the PNCs get larger with decrease of particle size whereas the tensile stress becomes smaller. For CD-2.5 with size comparable to the Kuhn segment length, the well-dispersed CD-2.5 induces the decrease of complex viscosity and reptation relaxation time, and thus results in an intermediate tensile stress and reduced elongation at break. The possible origins of the different reinforce mechanism were explained and it is inferred that whether the existence of a critical size of the nanoparticles for an effective reinforcement is still needed to be explored.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Glass transition temperature and the volume fraction of vitrified polymer
Rheological properties and the reptation relaxation time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The glass transition temperatures of PNCs filled with (a) CD-2.5, (b) CD-8.5, and (c) N330 at different volume fraction of the nanoparticles.
Plots of φ VP against φ NP for PNCs filled with (a) CD-2.5, (b) CD-8.5, and (c) N330 during the glass transition.

The linear rheological properties of PNCs filled with different volume fraction at 90 °C: (a,d,g) Cole-Cole curves; (b,e,h) complex viscosity (⎜η*⎜); (c,f,i) the reptation relaxation time (τr). Inset in Fig. 3c is the enlarged view of volume fraction below 15. (a–c) CD-2.5; (d–f) CD-8.5; (g-i)N330.
The plots of tensile properties against φ NP for PNCs filled with different nanoparticles. (a): Maximum tensile stress; (b): Elongation at break.

Schematic representation of chain conformations at the nanoparticle surface. (a) The dimensions of chain are expanded by CD-2.5, acting as the good solvent and there is only transient interaction between CDs and polymer chains. (b) The adsorbed chains with larger loops have fewer contact points with nanoparticle, leading to weaker interfacial interaction but can entangle with the adjacent chains. (c) The adsorbed chains with smaller loops have more contact points with nanoparticle, leading to stronger interfacial interaction, which can shield nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: Effect of nanoparticle size on the mechanical properties of polymer nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Rubber-Plastics, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science & Technology, Qingdao, 266042, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Rubber-Plastics, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science & Technology, Qingdao, 266042, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The mechanical properties of a series of polymer nanocomposites (PNCs) of polyvinylacetate (PVAc) and carbon dots (CDs) with average diameters of 2.5 and 8.5 nm (denoted as CD-2.5 and CD-8.5, respectively) or carbon black (N330) were investigated. The results show that when the nanoparticle size larger than the tube diameter of PVAc, e.g. N330 and CD-8.5, the complex viscosity, reptation relaxation time, and elongation at break of the PNCs get larger with decrease of particle size whereas the tensile stress becomes smaller. For CD-2.5 with size comparable to the Kuhn segment length, the well-dispersed CD-2.5 induces the decrease of complex viscosity and reptation relaxation time, and thus results in an intermediate tensile stress and reduced elongation at break. The possible origins of the different reinforce mechanism were explained and it is inferred that whether the existence of a critical size of the nanoparticles for an effective reinforcement is still needed to be explored.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer nanocomposites (PNCs) have been widely used in many areas [1–4]. It is well documented that the mechanical properties of polymer matrix can be significantly improved by incorporating of nanoparticles. This is mainly attributed to the attractive interaction between polymer chains and nanoparticles, leading to the formation of adsorbed polymer layers surrounding the nanoparticles. There is a slower mobility or even no mobility of polymer chains in the adsorbed layer, resulting in a higher modulus of the PNCs than that of the bulk polymer [5,6]. At higher filler loading, the adsorbed layers become the majority in polymer matrix and spread over the whole sample [7–10]. Therefore, the adsorbed layers play an important role in tuning the macroscopic properties of PNCs. It has been proved that the macroscopic properties of PNCs can be designed by adjusting the properties of the interfacial layer. There are many parameters affecting the properties of the adsorbed layer in PNCs, including nanoparticle size, molecular weight and chain rigidity of the polymer, particle-polymer interactions, distribution of the particles in the matrix, and so on [11–13]. Therefore, abundant works have been devoted to study the impact of nanoparticles on the performance PNCs. Among them, the effect of nanoparticle size has been found to exhibit huge influence on the mechanical properties of PCNs.

Generally, the reinforcement effect becomes stronger with the decrease of nanoparticle size. This rests on the larger specific surface area and increased number of smaller nanoparticles at the same loading, which allow more polymer chains or chain segments adsorbed on the particle surface, and as a result, lead to a higher content of vitrified polymer and stronger improvement of mechanical properties. However, it is noted that, with decline of nanoparticle size, the crowding of polymer chains or chain segments at the nanoparticle surface becomes serious and the adsorbed chains adjust conformations accordingly [14]. It has been reported that, at the same nanoparticle, the adsorbed chains of polymers with low molecular weight can pack compactly, leading to a larger density and much suppressed dynamics than the neat polymer, while the chains with high molecular weight are prone to form long loops and bridges between nanoparticles, resulting in a lower density and less confined dynamics than the neat polymer [15]. In this sense, similar compact packing situation for a single polymer at a larger nanoparticle surface is expected, whereas adsorbed layer with lower density and less confined dynamics will happen at a smaller nanoparticle surface. In other words, there will be fewer contact points of a polymer chain with smaller nanoparticles than with bigger ones, leading to weaker interfacial interaction. This has indeed been verified by recent researches, which demonstrate the existence of more contact points between the polymer chains and larger nanoparticles compared with smaller nanoparticles, resulting a longer desorption time of chains or chain segments at larger nanoparticle surface caused by stronger interfacial interaction strength [16–18]. Therefore, for nanoparticles with size far smaller than the gyration radius (Rg) of matrix polymer, there might be only transient interaction instead of contact points between polymer chains and tiny nanoparticles [19–23]. To our best knowledge, the influence of the aforementioned factors on the mechanical properties of PNCs is, however, relatively less considered [24–26].

Until now, most researches focus on the diffusion of attractive nanoparticles with different sizes in polymer melt [20,21,27,28]. It is considered that a core-shell mechanism dominates the suppressed diffusion of nanoparticles larger than the gyration radius (Rg), while a vehicle mechanism with polymer chains serving as carriers or vehicles is accepted to describe the fast diffusion of nanoparticles smaller than the tube diameter (dT) of the matrix polymers. The model emphasizes the effect of desorption time of the adsorbed chains or chain segments, which is determined by the number of contact points between polymer chain and nanoparticles, i.e., the interfacial interaction strength [18]. As reported, the ultra-small nanoparticles with size smaller than Rg and comparable to the Kuhn segment length can enlarge the dimensions of polymer chain and behave like the good solvent [29–31]. Meanwhile, there is only transient interaction between polymer chains and ultra-small nanoparticles. So the reinforcement mechanism of ultra-small nanoparticles should be different from that of larger nanoparticles. Moreover, it has been proposed by Zhang et al. [13] that a critical minimum size of reinforcing filler should exist, even though its specific size is still unclear.

In this work, we have studied the influence of nanoparticles with different size on the mechanical properties of polyvinylacetate (PVAc) nanocomposites and tried to make clear whether there is a critical size of nanoparticles for effective reinforcing the polymer matrix. To this end, two synthesized carbon dots (CDs) and commercial carbon black (N330) with average diameters ranging from 2.5 nm, comparable to the Kuhn segment length, to 30 nm, larger than Rg of PVAc, were used. The obtained results reveal a clear dependence of PNCs properties on the nanoparticle size but question the existence of a critical size of nanoparticles for an effective reinforcement. The aim of this paper to present the detailed experimental results and explain the possible origins of different reinforce mechanisms.

Polyvinylacetate (PVAc), with a weight-average molecular weight Mw = 150,000 g/mol, was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. Citric acid and urea was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd and Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, respectively. N330 with an average diameter of 30 nm was purchased from Degussa Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (China). Commercial ethyl acetate and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were used as received.

Amorphous carbon dots (CDs) with two different sizes were prepared by a cost-effective hydrothermal method according to protocols in the literature [32,33]. As presented in Fig. S1a, the CDs produced with only citric acid as carbon source exhibit a diameter ranging from 2–3 nm with an average diameter of 2.5 nm (thus denoted as CD-2.5) [32]. The CDs fabricated with citric acid and urea as the carbon sources (Fig. S1a’) were confirmed to have an average diameter of 8.5 nm (denoted as CD-8.5) [33]. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy results (Fig. S1b and b’) show that the surface of CD-2.5 is covered with plenty of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups, while some amide groups besides hydroxyl and carbonyl groups exist on the surface of the CD-8.5. The Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) results (Fig. S1c and c’) demonstrate the amorphous feature of the CDs.

Solution mixing was employed to prepare well-dispersed PNCs. First, different amounts of CD-2.5, CD-8.5 and N330 were dispersed in DMF and ultrasonicated for 1 h to obtain transparent solution. Then, PVAc of different weight were added and stirred until completely dissolved. After that, the mixed solutions were further ultrasonicated for another 1 h to avoid the reagglomeration of nanoparticles. Finally, the PVAc/nanoparticle solutions were poured into Teflon dishes and dried in a fume hood for 48 h at 100 °C. The as-cast films were transferred to a vacuum oven at 150 °C for 12 h in order to remove all the residual solvents in the film. The volume fraction of the CDs and N330 were calculated by mass conservation with the PVAc, where the densities of CD-2.5, CD-8.5 and N330 are estimated to be 1.17, 1.70 and 1.70 g/cm3, respectively. Finally, the volume fraction of both CDs in PNCs is 3.5, 7.1, 14.7, 27 and 40.8, respectively. And for N330, its volume fraction is 7.1, 14.7, 27 and 40.8, respectively.

The samples for rheological and mechanical tests were prepared by molding the milled PNCs at 90 °C under 5 MPa. The specimens with 25 mm in diameter and 1 mm in thickness were acquired and used for the rheological test. For the mechanical test, the molded PNCs films were cut using a custom steel-ruled die to make dumbbell specimens, whose dimensions in the center section are 12 mm long, 2 mm width and 180 ± 20 μm in thickness.

The samples for transmission electron microscopy observation were prepared by embedding the PNCs in epoxy resin, curing overnight and subsequently slicing into ultrathin sections of ∼100 nm in thickness through microtoming technique with the Leica EM UC7 using a diamond knife.

TEM observations were performed on a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope with an operating voltage of 200 kV. The Bruker VERTEX70 spectrometer was used for FTIR analysis. FTIR spectra in the wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm−1 were obtained by averaging 32 scans at a 4 cm−1 resolution. The 2D WAXD (Xeuss 2.0) measurements were performed at room temperature by using the Xenocs 2.0 equipment with incident X-ray Cu-Kα beam (λ = 1.5418 Å). The WAXD patterns were recorded by a 2-dimensional detector Pilatus 300 K at a sample-to-detector distance of 173 mm. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC 25, TA Instruments) was used to estimated glass transition temperature (Tg) and specific heat capacity (Cp). Measurements were carried out under a nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 K/min. The rheological measurements were performed on a strain-controlled rheometer (ARES-G2, TA Instruments) with 25 mm parallel-plate geometry in temperature range of 70–110 °C with an interval of 10 °C. The oscillation frequency sweeps from 0.01 to 100 rad/s were performed. The strain amplitudes were changed with temperature to ensure linear viscoelastic responses, which were determined by oscillation amplitude sweeps. Based on the time−temperature superposition (TTS), the master curves were built by superposing a series of frequency-dependent rheological curves in a manner of horizontal and vertical shifting at a reference temperature of 90 °C. The tensile tests were performed by using the INSTRON 5943 universal material testing machine at room temperature. Five specimens from each sample were tested at an extension rate of 50 mm/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glass transition temperature and the volume fraction of vitrified polymer
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the loading content dependent glass transition temperature (Tg) of PNCs with different nanoparticles. The original DSC curves are supplied in Fig. S2. It reveals clearly the different influences the CDs and N330 on the Tg of the PNCs. According to the results presented in Fig. 1, following issues should be addressed. First, the Tg of PVAc first decreases by incorporating a small amount of CD-2.5 or CD-8.5 with volume fraction less than 5 vol%, and then increases with increasing CD content. Nevertheless, the Tg variation of PVAc caused by CDs is less than 2 °C. This is different from the literature reports, where the Tg of PNCs filled with ultra-small particles (<10 nm) becomes obviously larger [23,34]. Second, the Tg of PVAc in the PVAc/N330 nanocomposites has an about ∼5 °C decrease regardless of the N330 amount. It also deviates from the literature report on common PNCs filled with relatively bigger particles, which shows almost no effect on the Tg of the polymer matrix [35]. This may be related to their different polarity, leading to the weak interfacial interaction [36].

To understand the different influence of the CDs and N330 on the Tg of PVAc, the three-phase model was used to illustrate the diverse phase components of the PNCs [37]. In the model, there are nanoparticle, free polymer and vitrified polymer phases. For vitrified polymer phase, the tightly adsorbed polymer chains on the nanoparticle surface do not participate in the glass transition. The chain segments in the free polymer phase share the glass transition, and its content can be estimated by the jump of specific heat capacity at the glass transition. The volume fraction of free polymer is calculated according to the equation:
(1)
φ FP = ΔC PNCs/ΔC P
In which, the ΔC PNCs and ΔC P stand for the jump of specific heat capacity of PNCs and polymer, respectively.

The volume fraction of vitrified polymer, φ VP, can be calculated from φ FP andφ NP (the volume fraction of nanoparticle) by:
(2)
φ VP = 1-φ FP-φ NP

Fig. 2 shows plots of φ VP against φ NP for different PNCs. It is clear that the change trends of φ VP versus φ NP are quite different for the PNCs with distinct nanoparticles, even for the CDs with diverse sizes. For PVAc/CD-2.5, the vitrified polymer phase is found only when the φ NP is 7.1 vol% and 14.7 vol% with a maximum value of reached about 15 vol% at φ NP = 7.1 vol%. In contrast, the φ VP of PVAc/CD-8.5 increases steadily with the rising φ NP up to 27 vol% with a maximum value of about 19 vol%. For PVAc/N330 nanocomposites, the φ VP also increases with φ NP like PVAc/CD-8.5 but with a much smaller value. The maximum of 8 vol% achieved at φ NP of 27 vol% is only 0.4 of the maximum value of PVAc/CD-8.5. As the φ VP is widely used as the predictor of interfacial interaction, and the larger the φ VP, the stronger the interfacial interaction between polymer and nanoparticle. The above experimental results suggest a stronger interfacial interaction between CD-8.5 and PVAc. The increment of φ VP with φ NP for PVAc/CDs-8.5 and PVAc/N330 nanocomposites is understandable since the decrease in nanoparticle size means an increment of specific surface area and number of nanoparticles at the same loading, and consequently more adsorbed polymer chains or chain segments on the particle surface. Under this consideration, the results for PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites are hard to be understood since even smaller CDs are used. This may be caused by special reinforcement mechanism and will be discussed latter.

The size of the cooperatively rearranging region (ξ) was widely used to characterize polymer segmental dynamics [38–40]. Generally, flexible polymer chains have higher packing efficiency, leading to a lower ξ, and which becomes larger with increase of the rigidity of polymer chains. In order to estimate the influence of nanoparticle on the polymer segmental dynamics, the ξ of all PNCs were calculated by the following formula according to the method reported in literatures [41,42].
(3)
V a
=
4 3
π
ξ a 3
=
k
T g 2
Δ
c p
ρ
(
δ T
)
2
c ‾
p 2
Where Va is the volume of a cooperatively rearranging region, k is the Boltzmann constant, ρ is the density,
c ‾
p
is the average of the extrapolated cp values at Tg,
c ‾
p
=(cp liquid + cp glass)/2, Δcp is the step height at Tg, and δT is the mean temperature fluctuation, estimated by using the equation δT = ΔT/2.5, where ΔT is defined as the temperature difference between 16% and 84% of the Δcp step height.

All the data of ξ are presented in Fig. S3. It can be clearly seen that the ξ of PNCs filled with CD-8.5 is the largest among the three kinds of PNCs. This means that the polymer segmental dynamics are strongly confined by the CD-8.5, which is in accordance with the data of φ VP and refers to a high rigidity of the polymer chains. For the other two kinds of PNCs, the smaller ξ values indicate that polymer segmental dynamics are less affected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological properties and the reptation relaxation time
ce:section.ce:para: Rheological measurement is another way to evaluate the polymer chain dynamics affected by nanoparticles and the structure/phase behavior of the nanocomposites. According to the TTS principle, master curves were constructed from several isothermal frequency sweeps at 90 °C and their related horizontal shift factors are presented in Fig. S4. The Cole-Cole curve, the frequency dependencies of complex viscosity (⎜η*⎜) and the reptation relaxation time of all PNCs are plotted in Fig. 3 . The Cole-Cole plot is always used to judge the phase-separation behavior of two-phase composites by characterizing the relaxation behavior of polymers [43,44]. Usually, the Cole−Cole curve would be a smooth semicircle shape when the two-phase composites have good compatibility. For the PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites (Fig. 3a–c), the Cole-Cole curves (Fig. 3a) are semicircle and out of shape with φ NP less and higher than 7.1 vol%, respectively. This indicates that the PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites are homogenous system at a lower CD content (≤7.1 vol%) with the CDs well dispersed in the polymer matrix. When the content of CD-2.5 is higher than 7.1 vol%, phase separation happens and the agglomerates of CDs begin to form. It is also noticed that the semicircle radius is smaller than that of neat PVAc, indicating the viscosity of PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites is lower than that of neat PVAc, which is in agreement with the data of ⎜η*⎜ shown in Fig. 3b. For the PVAc/CD-8.5 (Fig. 3d–f) and PVAc/N330 (Fig. 3g–i) nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 3d and g, the Cole-Cole curves are similar. The radius of semicircle is almost identical to that of neat PVAc when the φ NP is less than 3.5 vol% and 7.1 vol%, respectively. Phase separation takes place with further increase of the nanoparticle contents.

Fig. 3b shows the complex viscosity curves of PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites. The ⎜η*⎜of neat PVAc is constant at low frequencies and shows shear thinning behavior at higher frequencies. As expected, the ⎜η*⎜of PNCs at low frequencies is smaller than that of neat PVAc at low φ NP ≤ 7.1 vol%, and increases above that of neat PVAc at high φ NP. At higher frequencies, the ⎜η*⎜of all nanocomposites is smaller than that of neat PVAc, and the larger the φ NP, the more pronounced the shear thinning behavior. It is inferred that the CDs at low content act as plasticizer, lower the viscosity and accelerate the polymer dynamics. For the PVAc/CD-8.5 and PVAc/N330 nanocomposites shown in Fig. 3e and h, the ⎜η*⎜at low frequencies is nearly the same as that of neat PVAc when the φ NP is less than 3.5 vol% and 7.1 vol%, respectively. At high φ NP (>14.7 vol%), the ⎜η*⎜of all PNCs is larger than that of neat PVAc in the whole frequency range. The ⎜η*⎜of PVAc/CD-8.5 nacomposites is somewhat bigger than that of PVAc/N330 nanocomposites as the φ NP ranges from 3.5 vol% to 14.7 vol%, which consists with their higher values of φ VP and ξ, leading to slower chain dynamics and larger viscosities. However, when the φ NP is larger than 27 vol%, the ⎜η*⎜of PVAc/N330 nanocomposites surpasses that of PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites, which is further proved by their storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G’’) spectra, specified in Fig. S5. For PVAc/N330 nanocomposites (φ NP = 27 vol%), the G′ and G’’ of the composites are strongly enhanced compared to that of neat PVAc, and the G′ in the intermediate frequency region exhibits a power law scaling: G∼ω0.35, which indicates that the PNCs behave like the gel [8,34]. For PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites with φ NP = 40.8%, the G′ and G’’ are slightly increased and the spectra shift to the lower frequency region compared to that of neat PVAc.

The influence of nanoparticles on the polymer chain dynamics was also analyzed from the reptation relaxation time (τr), which is the longest relaxation time of polymer and is defined as the crossover point of the storage and loss moduli at low frequency: τr = 2π/ωG’ = G’’ [23]. The τr of PVAc/CD-2.5, PVAc/CD-8.5 and PVAc/N330 nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3c, f and i, respectively. Similar to the changes of ⎜η*⎜, for PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites, the τr decreases at low φ NP caused by the well dispersed CDs and becomes bigger than that of neat polymer at high φ NP. It reconfirms that the well dispersed CDs work as plasticizer and accelerate the reptation dynamics, leading to a lower τr. At high CD content, CDs aggregate into clusters and behave as hard sphere, resulting in the larger τr. The phenomenon is similar to the results of polymer/Untethered polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (POSS) nanocomposites [30,45]. For PVAc/CD-8.5 and PVAc/N330 nanocomposites, the τr increases with the rising φ NP, and is bigger for PVAc/CD-8.5 compared to that of PVAc/N330 nanocomposites at the same filler loading below 14.7 vol%, which further confirms the results of φ VP and ξ. It should be noted that when the φ NP is above 27 vol%, the τr of PVAc/N330 nanocomposites is larger than the experimental time so that it cannot be calculated.

Fig. 4 shows the tensile properties of PNCs filled with different volume fractions. For the maximum tensile stress, by careful comparison, PVAc/N330 nanocomposites have the largest values, while PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites are of the smallest values. The tensile stress should be even smaller for PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites, but in fact, it lies between those of PVAc/CD-8.5 and PVAc/N330 nanocomposites, which reaches a maximum at φNP = 3.5 vol% and then declines. This infers a different reinforcement mechanism of CD-2.5.

At the same φ NP, the elongation at break of PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites is bigger than that of PVAc/N330 nanocomposites. While for PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites, the elongation at break reaches a minimum at φ NP = 7.1 vol% and then increases with φ NP until 27 vol%. Meanwhile, the tensile stress and elongation at break of nanocomposites are deteriorated at higher filler loading, as the nanoparticles aggregate into clusters, accompanied by lots of defects, which is confirmed by TEM observation (Fig. S6).

As the nanoparticle sizes range from above the Rg to comparable to the Kuhn segment length, the PNCs show different mechanical properties. Importantly, it is to make clear the effect of nanoparticle with different size. Inspired by the model proposed by Yamamoto et al. [20], in which for nanoparticle larger than the Rg, its suppressed diffusion can be described by core-shell mechanism, while for nanoparticle smaller than the dT, the vehicle mechanism dominates its faster diffusion. In conjunction with our data, the Rg of the used PVAc is calculated according to equation:
(4)
R g
=
b
C ∞
×
(
M w
/
M
0 )
6
Where b is the monomer length of 0.43 nm for PVAc [46], C∞ is the Flory's characteristic ratio with a value of 8.79 [47], Mw and M0 are the weight-averaged molecular weight and molar mass of a monomer unit, respectively. Therefore, the Rg of PVAc is about 21.7 nm. According to the literature, the packing length (p) of PVAc is 0.37 nm [48], and the tube diameter obeys the relation dT = 17.68p = 6.5 nm [49].

It is found that the core-shell model is suitable to describe the reinforcement mechanism of N330 larger than Rg∼21.7 nm and CD-8.5 smaller than Rg but larger than dT∼6.5 nm. Generally, with the decrease of nanoparticle size, the φ VP becomes bigger at the same loading, which has been confirmed by experimental data. As the smaller nanoparticles are of larger specific surface area and number at the same loading. Meanwhile, it should be mentioned that the conformations of adsorbed chains may play an important effect on the φ VP. As reported, the adsorbed chains with different molecular weight are of distinctive conformations [15,50]. For the adsorbed chains with larger molecular weight, they are prone to form long loops and bridges between nanoparticles in order to alleviate entropy penalty. A recent research shows the larger the size of chain loop, much further its influence can spread [51], since the bigger chain loops can entangle with adjacent chains and trap them. Based on above researches, for CD-8.5 with a smaller size compared to N330, its adsorbed chains should be of larger loops and have fewer contact points, which also contribute to the content of φ VP, as depicted in Fig. 5 . Therefore, the PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites have bigger φ VP and ξ compared with PVAc/N330 nanocomposites at the same loading.

Moreover, it is expected that the ⎜η*⎜and τr of PVAc/CD-8.5 nacomposites are also much bigger, but surpassed by PVAc/N330 nanocomposites when the φ NP is larger than 27 vol%. It is speculated that the nanoparticle-polymer network spreads over the whole nanocomposites at higher φ NP > 27 vol%, dominating the rheological properties of the composites. Recently, simulations reveal that there are more number of contact points between larger nanoparticles and polymer chains, and so the interfacial interaction strength is much stronger and the desorbed time of adsorbed chains is much bigger [16,18]. Owing to the larger interfacial interaction strength of PVAc/N330 nanocomposites (φ NP = 27 vol%), the adsorbed polymer chains are highly confined and have larger relaxation time exceeding the experimental time. Therefore, the composites behave as the gel and have larger ⎜η*⎜. However, for PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites (φ NP = 40.8 vol%) of the weaker interfacial interaction, the desorption time of adsorbed chains is smaller than the reptation relaxation time, and so the composites exhibit terminal flow in the low frequency range and have smaller ⎜η*⎜. Similar behavior has been reported by senses et al., who also commented that the gel-like response was commonly observed for large attractive particles at such high volume fractions, but the difference was not explained [31].

Furthermore, based on the reinforcement models [52,53], the filler-rubber network plays an important role in the rubber reinforcement, which is closely related to the interfacial interaction strength and the slippage of polymer chains on the nanoparticle surface during tension. So, the PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites should have smaller tensile stress and larger elongation at break due to their weaker interfacial interaction strength, accompanied with reduced desorbed time, where adsorbed chains are easy to slip. Therefore, it should been emphasized that the larger φ VP does not mean a larger tensile stress, as the PVAc/N330 nanocomposites with a lower φ VP have the biggest tensile stress. We can conclude that the difference of tensile stress between PVAc/N330 and PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites is mainly determined by the interfacial interaction strength. Kritikos et al. pointed out the nanosheet mobility is another important parameter affecting the mechanical properties of PNCs. The larger graphene oxide sheets, leading to slower mobility of adsorbed chains and longer desorbed time, result in a higher modulus of PNCs compared to the smaller graphene oxide sheets [18]. For our systems, the mobility of N330 should be much slower and its effect on the modulus of PNCs is much stronger. It is speculated that the interfacial interaction strength is another presentation of mobility of nanoparticles.

For CD-2.5, which is smaller than dT, based on vehicle mechanism, there are no contact points but only transient interaction between polymer chains and nanoparticles, as presented in Fig. 5. Actually, there is vitrified polymer phase existing when the φ NP is 7.1 vol% and 14.7 vol%, which is also different from the recent reports that the step of specific heat capacity remains unchanged for P2VP/octaaminophenyl polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (OAPS) nanocomposites [9,28,34]. In addition, CDs have little influence on the Tg of PNCs, while the Tg of P2VP/OAPS nanocomposites increases up to 35 °C at φ NP = 54%. As explained, the mobility of OAPS is closely coupled to the segmental dynamics, and even they can relax cooperatively. Although transient interaction also exists between polymer chains and CD-2.5, it is anticipated that the mobility of CD-2.5 is less coupled to the segmental dynamics and to some extent CDs act as obstacles due to its larger size and higher weight. The research showes the dimensions of polymer chain in PNCs can be altered by nanoparticles with diameter smaller than Rg of polymer chain. At low φ NP, the polymer chains are expanded, and above a critical φ NP, the chain dimensions begin to decrease, because the formation of agglomerates results in lower effective volume fraction of nanoparticle [54].

Therefore, we infer that the vitrified polymer is generated by segment confinement, where the segmental dynamics are strongly restricted by adjacent CDs. Assuming CDs are individually dispersed, the interparticle distance (h) is estimated by the relation: h/D=(φm/φNP)-1, where D is the particle diameter and φm is the maximum random packing volume fraction with a value of 0.638. The h is 2.7 nm and 1.6 nm, respectively, when the φNP is 7.1 vol% and 14.7 vol%. As known, the Kuhn segment length of PVAc is 1.36 nm [47], so PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites should have the largest φ VP at φNP = 14.7 vol%, but owing to the formed aggregates making larger h and the enlarged tube diameter leading to real Kuhn segment length larger than 1.36 nm, the φ VP becomes smaller. At φNP = 7.1 vol%, the segmental dynamics are strongly confined by well dispersed CD-2.5, resulting in the largest φ VP and chain dimensions testified by tensile experiments. The molecule dispersed CDs are also able to enhance dynamics and induce the decrease of ⎜η*⎜and τr, because of the disentanglement effect and introduced free volume. However, P2VP/OAPS nanocomposites show opposite effect, whose viscosities increase with the rising of OAPS loading [28]. It is inferred that besides the strong interfacial interaction favorable of OAPS dispersion, its smaller size and hollow structure are indispensable for the cooperative motion of OAPS and chain segment, leading to the rising viscosities. The influences of CD-2.5 on the tensile properties are also distinctive. It is interesting to find that the tensile stress is larger than that of PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites but smaller than that of PVAc/N330 nanocomposites. While the elongation at break decreases to a minimum at φNP = 7.1 vol% and rises until the φNP is 27 vol%. As there is only transient interaction between polymer chains and nanoparticle, and higher nanoparticle concentration leads to larger transient interaction and bigger tensile stress. On the other hand, CD-2.5 acts as plasticizer, enlarging the tube diameter (dT) and Rg, which can decrease the entanglement density of polymer chains. It is, therefore, concluded that these effects work together to decide the tensile stress of nanocomposites. Furthermore, the critical φ NP is estimated to be about 7.1 vol%, and at which the polymer chains are highly expanded and have the largest dimensions. So, the elongation at break is the smallest at φ NP = 7.1 vol%, and above the critical φ NP, the polymer chains begin to collapse, leading to higher elongation at break.

The reinforcement mechanism of CD-2.5 are also different from early reports, in which the tensile strength and break elongation show the coincident trend with each other and the reason for the elevated ductility was attributed to the disentanglement effect produced by POSS [26]. Therefore, it is inferred that although the chain dimensions are expanded by both fillers, the mobility of CD-2.5 is much slower than that of POSS, behaving as obstacle to some extent which is bad for ductility. Finally, as claimed by Zhang et al., is there a critical minimum reinforced filler size? And they speculated that critical minimum size of the reinforced filler is probably the thermodynamically stable filler in the form of granular particles [13]. We think that the size of CD-2.5 should be larger than that of thermodynamically stable filler. Based on our data, although there is no adsorbed layer on the surface of CD-2.5, the mechanical properties of PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites are still strongly improved. Therefore, we think the claim is still an open question.

In summary, it is proved that PNCs with variable nanoparticle size have different mechanical properties. For CD-8.5 and N330, their reinforcement mechanism can be described in core-shell model. Compared to PVAc/N330 nanocomposites, the volume fraction of vitrified polymer is larger for PVAc/CD-8.5 nanocomposites and leads to the larger complex viscosity and reptation relaxation time, but they have smaller tensile stress and larger elongation at break. All these are belonged to the difference of adsorbed chain conformations, because the adsorbed chains on the CD-8.5 surface have larger loops, which can trap more adjacent polymer chains, and fewer contact points. This results in the weaker interfacial interaction and facilitates the slippage of polymer chains. For CD-2.5, based on vehicle mechanism, we think the mobility of CDs is less coupled to the segmental dynamic. So the molecule dispersed CDs not only induce the decrease of ⎜η*⎜and τr because of the disentanglement effect but also bring in the segment confinement, which results in the vitrified polymer and the reduced elongation at break. The tensile stress of PVAc/CD-2.5 nanocomposites lies between those of PVAc/CD-8.5 and PVAc/N330 nanocomposites, mainly determined by the transient interaction strength and entanglement density of polymer. Finally, is there a critical minimum reinforced filler size? We think it is still an open question.

Hao Zhang: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hanqi Zhu: Data curation, Software. Chunning Xu: Data curation, Software. Yunpeng Li: Data curation, Software. Qiang Liu: Methodology. Shaojuan Wang: Validation. Shouke Yan: Writing-review&editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Foundation of Key Laboratory of Rubber-Plastics (no. KF2020010), Ministry of Education/Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Rubberplastics, Qingdao University of Science and Technology.
